serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of bánh tráng trộn is approximately 200 g, typically served in a bag or on a plate as a street-food snack or light meal.

nutrient_source.title: Bánh tráng trộn — component-based nutrient estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for bánh tráng trộn as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted average of primary ingredients: dried rice paper (~40%), eggs (~15%), dried shrimp (~8%), green mango (~10%), peanuts (~7%), herbs and vegetables (~10%), dressing components (~10%). USDA SR Legacy entries used for rice paper (FDC ID 168917), eggs (FDC ID 748967), dried shrimp (FDC ID 175180), peanuts (FDC ID 172430), green mango (FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values represent a moderate-confidence estimate; confidence set to medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — rice-based dishes and rice paper analogues; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bánh tráng trộn. GI estimated at 53 based on the closest analogous food: plain rice paper / rice noodle dishes with mixed ingredients (GI ~50–56 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables). The presence of protein (eggs, dried shrimp), fat (peanuts), fibre (vegetables, green mango), and acidic dressing (lime, tamarind) collectively attenuate the glycaemic response relative to plain rice. GL per 200 g serving = (53 × 27.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 29.2, rounded to 28.6 using adjusted serving carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~840 mg per 200 g serving) from dried shrimp, soy sauce, and savoury dressing
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced soy sauce and dried shrimp to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (~27.5 g per 100 g; ~55 g per 200 g serving) from rice paper base, with low-to-moderate GI (~53) partially attenuated by protein, fat, and fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to approximately 100–150 g, monitor total carbohydrate intake, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to further blunt the postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate sodium (~420 mg/100 g) and moderate fat content (~5.8 g/100 g) including saturated fat (~1.1 g/100 g) from eggs, dried shrimp, and peanuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose a smaller portion (~100 g), request less soy sauce and dried shrimp to reduce sodium, and note that the unsaturated fat from peanuts is relatively heart-neutral when consumed in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bánh tráng trộn (Vietnamese: bánh tráng trộn, literally 'mixed rice paper') is a popular Vietnamese street food originating in Ho Chi Minh City and now widely served across Vietnam and in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore. The dish is made by tearing dried rice paper sheets into bite-sized pieces and tossing them with a vibrant mixture of ingredients including boiled quail eggs or chicken eggs, dried shrimp, shredded green mango or green papaya, Vietnamese coriander (rau răm), spring onion, fried shallots, roasted peanuts, fresh chilli, and a tangy-savoury dressing of tamarind, satay sauce, soy sauce, and lime juice. The result is a complex interplay of textures — chewy rice paper, crunchy peanuts, tender egg — and flavours — sour, salty, spicy, and umami. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate carbohydrate from the rice paper base (~27.5 g/100 g), meaningful protein from eggs and dried shrimp (~7.2 g/100 g), and moderate fat from peanuts (~5.8 g/100 g). Sodium is a notable concern at approximately 420 mg/100 g due to the soy sauce and dried shrimp. The glycaemic index is estimated at 53 (low-to-moderate), attenuated by the protein, fat, fibre, and acidic dressing components. Clinically, portion control is advised for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The dish contains common allergens including eggs, shellfish (dried shrimp), peanuts, and soy. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified food without specific preparation adjustments.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for bánh tráng trộn as a composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted ingredient composition using USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for individual components. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice paper and mixed rice-based dish data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates; no direct approved-source entry for bánh tráng trộn exists. Values should be updated if a direct HPB or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry becomes available. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for the primary components of this dish and is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA for this food type. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for the vegetable and herb components, and the USDA SR Legacy values for dried shrimp and rice paper do not provide a reliable composite figure. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in all component sources — no industrial trans fats present in traditional preparation. 5. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice paper and mixed rice-based dishes; direct GI measurement for bánh tráng trộn is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The input record listed cuisine as 'Singapore'; this has been corrected to 'Vietnamese' as bánh tráng trộn is a Vietnamese dish served in Singapore. Multilingual naming follows the Vietnamese cuisine rule (Vietnamese native-script name mandatory) plus Singaporean serving-context rules (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). 7. Halal status is marked false as dried shrimp sourcing and preparation cannot be confirmed halal without restaurant-specific certification. 8. The two restaurants listed in the input (Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant variants) are noted as the Singapore source context.
